items.text: Man does not live by murder alone... he needs affection, approval, encouragement and, occasionally, a hearty meal. Tonight, you have provided me with three... out of four. Anxiety strangled my appetite!
items.context: Opening remarks in which Hitchcock humorously acknowledged the honors being bestowed upon him while maintaining his characteristic wit about human psychology and fear.
items.publicReaction: The speech was well-received for its blend of humor, self-deprecation, and gratitude, with the closing tribute to his wife becoming the most memorable part.
items.source: AFI Life Achievement Award (Irving G. Thalberg Memorial Award) ceremony, American Film Institute

items.text: I am a typed director. If I made Cinderella, the audience would immediately be looking for a body in the coach.
items.context: Hitchcock discussing how his personal brand and reputation shaped audience expectations for any film he made, regardless of genre.
items.publicReaction: This statement became widely cited to explain Hitchcock's recognition as an auteur and how public perception of a director's style influences audience reception.
items.source: Wikiquote sources indicate publication in multiple interviews and writings, though original source requires verification

items.text: In the documentary the basic material has been created by God, whereas in the fiction film the director is a God; he must create life.
items.context: Distinction between documentary and narrative filmmaking, reflecting on the director's role and responsibility in creating fictional worlds.
items.publicReaction: This statement became influential in film theory and philosophy, establishing Hitchcock as a theorist of the director's creative power.
items.source: Attributed to Hitchcock interviews and writings on film theory

items.text: Nobody has a sense of humor anymore.
items.context: Complaint made during the release of Frenzy in 1972, expressing Hitchcock's observation about contemporary culture and sensibilities.
items.publicReaction: The statement generated discussion about cultural changes and generational differences in humor appreciation.
